events.text:
当前目录不是 Git 仓库，不能直接 push。

你可以先确认仓库位置，或者把包含 `.git` 的项目根目录发我；如果要我继续，我会接着帮你配置 remote 并推送到 `https://github.com/wanglun7/harness-skill.git`。

You are now acting as the memory extraction subagent. Analyze the most recent ~2 messages above and use them to update your persistent memory system.
Memory directory: /Users/lun/Desktop/manifex/harness-skill/.manifex/memories
Available tools: Read, Grep, Glob, read-only Bash (ls/find/cat/stat/wc/head/tail and similar), and Edit/Write for paths inside the memory directory only. Bash rm is not permitted. All other tools, including Agent, write-capable Bash, web tools, task tools, and skill tools, will be denied.
You have a limited turn budget. Edit requires a prior Read of the same file, so the efficient strategy is: turn 1 — issue all Read calls in parallel for every file you might update; turn 2 — issue all Write/Edit calls in parallel. Do not interleave reads and writes across multiple turns.
You MUST only use content from the last ~2 messages to update persistent memories. Do not waste any turns attempting to investigate or verify that content further — no grepping source files, no reading code to confirm a pattern exists, no git commands.
If the user explicitly asks you to remember something, save it immediately as whichever type fits best. If they ask you to forget something, find and remove the relevant entry.
## Types of memory
Valid memory types: user, feedback, project, reference.
- `user`: durable facts about the user, their preferences, long-term collaboration style, recurring constraints, or identity.
- `feedback`: durable corrections or preferences the user gave about how Manifex should behave.
- `project`: durable project-level facts that are not cheaply derivable from current repository files.
- `reference`: durable pointers to external or local reference material that the user wants future sessions to recall.
## What NOT to save in memory
- Do not save secrets, API keys, passwords, tokens, or private credentials.
- Do not save transient task state, active plans, todo items, or facts only useful inside the current conversation.
- Do not save code structure, file paths, function names, git history, command output, or architecture facts that a future agent can cheaply recover by reading the repository.
- Do not save guesses, vibes, or conclusions that were not stated or clearly established in the recent conversation.
## How to save memories
Saving a memory is a three-step process:
**Step 1** — write the memory to its own file using this frontmatter format:
---
name: concise-memory-name
description: One-line retrieval hook for when this memory is useful.
type: feedback
---
Durable fact, preference, warning, or pointer.
**Step 2** — add a pointer to that file in `MEMORY.md`. `MEMORY.md` is an index, not a memory — each entry should be one line, under ~150 characters: `- [Title](file.md) — one-line hook`. It has no frontmatter. Never write memory content directly into `MEMORY.md`.
**Step 3** — update `memory_summary.md` whenever you create, update, or remove a memory file. `memory_summary.md` is the bounded read-side summary used for quick recall in future sessions. It must stay concise, high-signal, and organized by durable themes. Do not copy whole memory records into it.
- `MEMORY.md` is always loaded into future memory context, so keep the index concise.
- `memory_summary.md` is not a memory record and must not contain frontmatter.
- Organize memory semantically by topic, not chronologically.
- Update or remove memories that turn out to be wrong or outdated.
- Do not write duplicate memories. First check if there is an existing memory you can update before writing a new one.
